George Lucas, great? Really? Sure he came up with a pretty amazing take on the classic heroes journey, but then he started doing things like change the name of Revenge of the Jedi to Return of the Jedi because one less letter in the title increases profit margins x% in merchandise sales. I'll give him A New Hope and Empire, and shoot, even RoTJ (I can forgive the ewoks because they were pretty damn cool when I saw them as a 7 year old on the big screen in 1983, but everything after... meh. Disney however, gave us an amazing season of The Mandalorian, Rogue One (objectively the BEST SW movie), Rebels, Solo (it was a fun movie, and I feel like people are coming around to that fact 8 years later, I don't care what the haters say) and the most amazing piece of SW media full stop, not to mention probably one of the best Television Shows of all time in Andor. Sure, they make missteps, and their transparent, wholly profit driven pandering to the LGBTQ community is insulting, but they've made some amazing media out of a tired franchise (mostly by steering away from the "central" saga of the most specialest boy in the galaxy. Let's face it, even in the Lucas Era, the best SW stories were all, say it with me now, EU stories.

@DADON99  Except the formula has successfully changed in this instance. UFC and WWE are two completely different industries. The objective of a pro wrestling card is to draw feelings out of the crowd and take them on an emotional rollercoaster with dips and highs. Booking the order of a UFC card is nothing like that. Having a big match as the opener starts the show off with a bang and not saturating the end with multiple big matches ensures the crowd doesn't burn out by the final match and it's not like it's something they just started doing. It's been a thing for a while now. The way WWE is currently booking the order of the card maintains viewer interest a lot better, so it makes perfect sense. Jey/Gunther was definitely WrestleMania worthy and Triple H knows it. That's why he obviously booked the match to happen at that show, but it wasn't going to be the main event on a show that had Roman/Punk/Rollins. Jey isn't pretending to be Cena at all. He's his own man and is clearly over. That matters far more than what some random hater says. Gunther had a great reign as Champ. Have there been better? Sure, but that doesn't take away from his accomplishments. It's well documented that Hogan wasn't utilized all the time. He was presented as an attraction and therefore it was smart business not to as to make it a bigger deal when he makes appearances which is the same thing they do with Roman Reigns and it's working. "These dudes" do wrestle and still do things of meaning and interest when they're not wrestling here and there when it makes sense to. Just because you say otherwise doesn't make it valid. Roman was around more often during the start of his heel run to lay the story out. Now he's over and they don't need him on every single show. Have you been living under a rock? In general, the audience these days are fickle and when a top star becomes overexposed, they turn on them. It's not like the days of Hogan anymore when the fans just accepted the on-screen story for what it is. I can understand your point that there are a lot less wrestlers these days that prioritize the entertainment aspect of pro wrestling. I'm not forgetting anything about LA Knight, but it seems you forgot the context in which I was talking about him. It can't be denied that Knight is over, but Jey has been on a hot streak, has the crowds going crazy for him and has been selling merch like hotcakes. Combine that with the fact that Jey winning the World Title is the culmination of his story as the Right Hand Man to Roman finally becoming a standout singles star. Having that story conclude at Mania made sense. It doesn't mean that also isn't in the future of LA Knight. I'm capable of being patient. The only kinds of people who complain about not getting what they want when they want it are those who are self-entitled and have a short attention span. Nakamura held the US Title not so long ago. While he could certainly be booked better, he's far from a jobber as jobbers don't win titles.

@RonWeassly Here's the issue Ron, and we'll use The Walking Dead as a prime example. When the camera's off & production is shut down till next year. It's no longer Daryl Dixon & Rick Grimes - they are now Norman Reedus & Andrew Lincoln.

When it's Wrestling, when the camera is off - They are still pro wrestlers until they are in their car, behind closed doors & away from you, me & the rest of the fans. When you see them in the Arena or in public, you're not calling them by their real government name. You believe they are them on TV, and they are currently on an ongoing series that never ends with storylines that could last a full year, even more. Folks are intrigued to see John Cena beat up Cody Rhodes, believe that he's hates him. Folks wanna see Seth Rollins run through Roman & Punk because, they believe that hate, however, if you uncover all your tricks, and show the gameplan & show these guys hanging out at a Dave & Busters while their story is running. What is the point, the magic & believably is gone. It's one thing when it's 20-30 years removed on a podcast & no longer an active wrestler, but folks just want everything spoiled by the dirt sheets, so what's the point anyone?

I mean, are you that excited to see the Rabbit pulled out of the hat anymore, once they pull you aside & show you how it's done? I mean, are you going to pay to keep seeing it again & again when the magic is gone?

That's why it's a bad idea, and it sucks worse the reason why they are doing it, is because they are hoping to get an Emmy for this. Why play the characters & act them out in public if you show everyone once again - how the sausage is made.

The difference is, as we will reference The Walking Dead, for that show - when the cameras are off, and they are done filming the season & wait till next year for the next, it's no longer Daryl Dixon & Rick Grimes - it's Norman Reedus & Andrew Lincoln. The shows don't try to push any sort of believably, as it's based in fiction that it's unlikely a Zombie Apocalypse will ever happen. 

WWE or any other wrestling promotion, is based on reality & pushing the believably of it's wrestlers to have real life grudges to force them into storylines and headlining matches making the consumer believe that they hate one another & wanting you to see them kick the crap out of each other. Their stories are ongoing, 24/7, pushing the belief. When the cameras off & the shows are over. Phil Brooks is still CM Punk in public, until he able to get in his car, behind closed doors or at home. He puts on the facade to keep engagement & us believing. It's one thing when a wrestler like The Undertaker, Stone Cold talk about their exploits behind the scenes on a podcast because they are 20-30 years removed & their stories are over, but it's another thing when we just had Cody Rhodes VS John Cena at Wrestlemania, and the next night, you are watching WWE Unreal, where they explain to you the behind the scenes of how they crafted the story & match beat by beat, basically destroying that believably going forward that John Cena isn't ruining wrestling, and he doesn't hate the fans & he hangs out with Cody at a Waffle House. It just ruins the ongoing story they are locked into, that could run for a year or more. 

It's like this, you pay to see a Magic Show & then you get pulled aside & someone spoils for you how they pulled that rabbit out of the hat. With that knowledge, are you really going to pay to see that Magic Show again knowing all the tricks of the trade on how they did everything? It's just not as incredible as it was, the first time. Mentally, the value isn't there. That's what i'm getting at.

On PlutoTV, I’ve been catching up on 90s black sitcoms like Roc and Hanging with Mr. Cooper. 

Almost all of season 2 of Roc was filmed live in front of a studio audience and one of the actors would open up the show by talking about that night’s episode as they walk around the set. In the background you’d see some of the stars getting last minute make up and the camera men getting ready to film. 

Then, they made a joke or got a joke
told on them and then, they’d introduce the show. After a minute of the intro, whoever is supposed to be in a scene is there. And they flawlessly pulled off a great show. 

I’ve always viewed pro wrestling that way. A show that’s taped live before an audience that also gets people get to see it live at home. Thing is, I was just talking to someone who used to go to ruthless aggression era shows and they compared the  experience to a modern day show. 

They said that these days, you see the producers giving the wrestlers directions or telling one when to go attack someone during a segment. You basically see everything. 

There’s even a video of Jade Cargill, hiding next to the barricade right beside the announce table. She was waiting for her cue. Some lady tapped her on the shoulder, and Cargill went to attack Naomi.

He said that WWE used to hide this stuff well for immersion. Like the refs giving directions. He says it’s all just seen now.
